skill.name: scvi-tools
skill.description: Deep generative models for single-cell omics; use when you need probabilistic batch correction (scVI), transfer learning, uncertainty-aware differential expression, or multimodal integration (totalVI/MultiVI).
skill.summary: Deep generative models for single-cell omics analysis: probabilistic batch correction, transfer learning, and multimodal integration.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 395 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill contains only markdown documentation files with Python code examples for the scvi-tools library. 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' flags are markdown code fence delimiters around Python examples. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' flags are markdown '###' heading markers. 'Dynamic function constructor' is a mathematical ELBO formula. No executable scripts, no runtime network calls, and no environment variable access exist in this skill.

high_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm (false positive - markdown headings)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged '###' markdown heading markers as 'Weak cryptographic algorithm'. Each flagged location is a level-3 markdown heading (e.g., '### Output Column Descriptions', '### DestVI'). These are documentation section headings, not cryptographic references. All 47 instances across all files are FALSE POSITIVES.

medium_findings.title: Ruby/shell backtick execution (false positive - markdown code fences)
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer misinterpreted markdown triple-backtick code fences as Ruby shell backtick execution. All 345 flagged locations are markdown code blocks containing Python code examples for scvi-tools usage (model training, differential expression, data loading). No actual command execution occurs. All instances are FALSE POSITIVES.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance (false positive - code comments)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged documentation text mentioning 'system' or analysis terms as 'System reconnaissance'. Flagged lines are Python code comments (e.g., '# Use change mode to avoid finding tiny effects') and documentation descriptions (e.g., 'Rapid analysis of new datasets'). No reconnaissance intent. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL (false positive - source repository link)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged the source repository URL in SKILL.md. This is a legitimate attribution link to the GitHub repository, not a malicious network endpoint or data exfiltration target. FALSE POSITIVE.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown code blocks misinterpreted as command execution
dangerous_patterns.description: All 345 'external_commands' findings are markdown code fences (triple backticks) containing Python code examples for the scvi-tools library. The static analyzer incorrectly classified these as Ruby shell backtick execution. The skill contains no executable scripts, only documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown heading markers misinterpreted as weak cryptography
dangerous_patterns.description: All 47 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are markdown '###' heading markers. These are level-3 documentation headings, not cryptographic algorithm references. The scanner pattern-matched '###' without understanding context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mathematical formula misinterpreted as dynamic function constructor
dangerous_patterns.description: The 'Dynamic function constructor' finding at theoretical-foundations.md:191 is a mathematical formula showing the ELBO (Evidence Lower Bound) loss function: L = E_q[log p(x|z)] - KL(q(z|x) || N(0,I)). This is a standard variational inference equation in a markdown code block.

dangerous_patterns.title: Source repository URL flagged as network risk
dangerous_patterns.description: The 'Hardcoded URL' finding at SKILL.md:7 is the legitimate GitHub source repository link. This is standard attribution and has no runtime network behavior.

content.user_title: Analyze single-cell omics data with scvi-tools
content.value_statement: Single-cell omics analysis across experiments and modalities is challenging due to batch effects and technical noise. This skill provides expert guidance on using scvi-tools deep generative models for probabilistic batch correction, multimodal integration, and uncertainty-aware differential expression.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup and training of scVI models for scRNA-seq batch correction and latent representation learning
Explains Bayesian differential expression analysis with effect size thresholds and posterior uncertainty quantification
Provides model selection guidance for multimodal data (CITE-seq, multiome) and spatial transcriptomics
Covers transfer learning with scANVI for semi-supervised cell annotation and reference mapping
Details probabilistic modeling foundations including variational inference, ELBO optimization, and VAE architectures
limitations: Requires existing AnnData objects with preprocessed count data; does not perform raw data loading or sequence alignment
GPU setup, environment configuration, and package installation must be handled outside this skill
Does not cover non-scvi-tools analysis methods or comprehensive Scanpy workflow pipelines
Model training time and convergence guidance depends on dataset size and available hardware

use_cases.title: Batch correction for multi-batch scRNA-seq studies
use_cases.description: A researcher combines scRNA-seq data from multiple experiments and batches. This skill guides them through using scVI for probabilistic integration, latent space correction, and verifying batch mixing.

use_cases.title: Multimodal integration of CITE-seq data
use_cases.description: An analyst needs to jointly model RNA and protein measurements from CITE-seq. The skill describes totalVI model setup, training, and extraction of joint latent representations.

use_cases.title: Uncertainty-aware differential expression
use_cases.description: A scientist needs to find genes that change between conditions with confidence estimates. This skill explains Bayesian DE using posterior distributions and effect size thresholds.

prompt_templates.title: Setup and train scVI model
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through setting up and training an scVI model on my scRNA-seq AnnData object. I have batch information in adata.obs['batch']. Show me the steps from setup_anndata to extracting the latent representation.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user setting up scVI batch correction

prompt_templates.title: Run Bayesian differential expression
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me run Bayesian differential expression between cell types A and B using my trained scVI model. Use change mode with delta 0.25 and explain how to interpret the results.

prompt_templates.title: Multimodal integration with totalVI
prompt_templates.prompt: I have CITE-seq data with RNA and protein measurements in my AnnData object. Help me set up and train a totalVI model for joint analysis of both modalities.
prompt_templates.scenario: User working with multimodal single-cell data

prompt_templates.title: Semi-supervised annotation with scANVI
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a labeled reference dataset and an unlabeled query dataset. Guide me through using scANVI for transfer learning and label propagation to annotate the query cells.
prompt_templates.scenario: User mapping new data onto an existing reference

output_examples.input: How do I correct batch effects in my scRNA-seq data?
output_examples.output: A step-by-step explanation of scVI for batch correction: preparing AnnData with raw counts, setting up the model with batch_key for batch covariate registration, training the model, extracting corrected latent representations with get_latent_representation(), and verifying integration quality by checking batch mixing in UMAP visualization.

output_examples.input: Which scvi-tools model should I use for CITE-seq data?
output_examples.output: A comparison of model options: totalVI for paired RNA+protein from the same cells, guidance on setting up the model with protein_expression_key for protein data specification, and explanation of how totalVI jointly models both modalities in a shared latent space.

output_examples.input: How is differential expression different in scvi-tools compared to standard methods?
output_examples.output: An explanation of Bayesian DE advantages: posterior distributions instead of point estimates, configurable effect size thresholds (delta) for practical significance control, built-in batch correction during DE testing, and posterior expected false discovery proportion control instead of traditional multiple testing correction.

best_practices: Always register raw count data with setup_anndata before training any scvi-tools model; never use log-normalized values as input
Use change mode with an appropriate delta threshold for differential expression to ensure practical significance over statistical significance alone
Verify integration quality by visualizing batch mixing in latent space with UMAP before proceeding to downstream analysis
anti_patterns: Do not use log-normalized expression values as model input; scvi-tools models expect raw counts for correct probabilistic modeling
Do not skip the setup_anndata step; covariate registration for batch and technical factors is required for proper batch correction
Do not train models with default parameters without monitoring convergence; adjust max_epochs and early stopping based on ELBO loss trends

faq.question: What data format does scvi-tools require?
faq.answer: scvi-tools works with AnnData objects and expects raw count data, not log-normalized values.

faq.question: Can scvi-tools run on a CPU?
faq.answer: Yes, scvi-tools can run on CPU. GPU acceleration is recommended for large datasets but not required.

faq.question: How is scVI different from standard batch correction methods?
faq.answer: scVI uses a deep generative model to learn a probabilistic latent representation, providing uncertainty quantification and handling complex technical variation.

faq.question: What is the change mode in differential expression?
faq.answer: Change mode tests whether the effect size exceeds a minimum threshold delta. This finds biologically meaningful changes rather than statistically significant small effects.

faq.question: How long does scVI training take?
faq.answer: Training time depends on dataset size and hardware. Small datasets train in minutes on CPU. Large datasets benefit significantly from GPU acceleration.

faq.question: Can I save and reload a trained model?
faq.answer: Yes. Use model.save() to save and model.load() to reload a trained scvi-tools model with the original AnnData object.
